family_audit.reason: Round 2/20 measured this family as a large PASS_LOSS at 30.286848 ms, with the hot-band kernel itself ballooning to 43.291456 ms and occupancy still capped by registers.
After the failed geometry pivot, the best next move is a bounded exploit pass on the last accepted PTX base rather than another broad family swing.
This remains the cheapest orthogonal probe on top of the PTX base if a tighter PTX exploit pass stalls.
The accepted PTX base at 24.419329 ms should stay available as the low-risk restore path while the loop explores small follow-on tweaks.
This family already recorded a loss before the PTX branch took over as base.
The 256x128 regression shows geometry was the problem this round; it does not provide new evidence for reopening the K32 stage-deepening family.

directions.name: Retune The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Hot-Band Control Path
directions.hypothesis: Round 2/20 falsified the 256x128 geometry pivot hard: end-to-end runtime regressed from the accepted 24.419329 ms PTX base to 30.286848 ms, and the hot-band kernel alone grew to 43.291456 ms even though registers fell to 168/thread. That means the best next move is not another wide family jump but a bounded exploit pass on top of the accepted PTX one-k 128x128 branch. The winning PTX base still had 200 registers per thread, only 16.62% active warps, 7.20% long-scoreboard stall, and 5.41% barrier stall, so there is still room to trim local control/export overhead without repeating the failed geometry experiment.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual control-path overhead and live-range pressure inside the accepted PTX one-k 128x128 hot-band branch.
directions.risk: Moderate. PTX microkernel changes are correctness-sensitive, but this stays on the last measured winner instead of introducing another new geometry family.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the accepted 24.419329 ms PTX base
ranking_notes: This matches the current highest-scoring open family representative in the frontier after the 256x128 loss.
It restores focus to the last accepted base instead of spending another round on a broad geometry hypothesis.
Keep the scope bounded to one PTX control-path improvement, not a fresh rewrite.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if one PTX-local retune cannot improve hot-band time or if it raises register pressure without recovering runtime.

directions.name: Retune Hot-Band CTA Traversal On The 128x128 PTX Grid
directions.hypothesis: After the failed 256x128 pivot, the safest remaining orthogonal test is a small traversal/locality probe on the proven PTX base. The accepted PTX branch still launches a 60x50 hot-band grid while only two 4-warp CTAs can stay resident per SM. A grouped-row or serpentine traversal tweak is cheap, reversible, and orthogonal to PTX inner-loop control retuning, so it stays alive as the next best explore family if the bounded PTX exploit path stalls.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and traversal efficiency on the current 128x128 PTX hot-band grid.
directions.risk: Low to moderate. The surface is small, but prior CTA-order experiments have shown mixed evidence, so the upside is limited.
ranking_notes: This is the best low-surface explore family still open after the geometry loss.
It remains below the PTX exploit path because the likely upside is smaller.
Use it only if the exploit pass does not recover anything.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if traversal changes leave hot-band time and cache-facing counters flat after one round.

directions.name: Restore The Accepted PTX One-K 128x128 Base
directions.hypothesis: The current source surface is the losing 256x128 branch, while the accepted PTX base at 24.419329 ms is already known-good and already beats CUTLASS. If the loop needs to recover a safe surface before any further exploration, routing the default hot-band launch straight back to the accepted PTX one-k branch is the lowest-risk move. This stays as the third direction because it does not push the frontier forward by itself, but it is still a valid option if the loop starts accumulating noisy losses.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Not a new bottleneck attack; this is the recovery path back to the accepted PTX base after the failed 256x128 excursion.
directions.risk: Low. This is a simple return to the last measured winner.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the accepted 24.419329 ms run
ranking_notes: Keep one explicit recovery candidate in the set because the current source is far off the accepted base.
This is intentionally ranked below forward-looking directions because it mostly preserves state instead of exploring new upside.
If round-to-round noise or build risk rises, this becomes the safety valve.
directions.stop_condition: Use this if the higher-ranked directions look too risky or if the loop needs to get back onto the accepted PTX base immediately.

notes: Round 3/20 human-review audit: the queue still contributes only the approval gate and the exactly-one-direction rule. The last round cleanly falsified the 256x128 pivot family as the current primary bet: it regressed to 30.286848 ms and slowed the hot-band kernel to 43.291456 ms. The search should therefore return to the accepted PTX base rather than compounding that loss with another broad family swing. Accepted as the primary family for this diagnosis is a bounded exploit pass on the active PTX one-k 128x128 control path. Deferred fallback families are the light traversal/locality retune on the PTX grid and the explicit restore to the accepted PTX branch. Rejected for this round are reusing the failed 256x128 pivot as primary, reopening the earlier standard one-k copy-cadence family, and reopening the K32 stage-deepening branch.
